title: Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines for Management of Sepsis and Septic Shock 2026
one_line_summary: The 2026 Surviving Sepsis Campaign guidelines provide updated, evidence-based recommendations for the acute and post-acute management of sepsis and septic shock, emphasizing early identification, judicious interventions, and patient-centered care.
key_pearls: Implement systematic screening (NEWS/NEWS2 over qSOFA) and "Sepsis Huddle" protocols for early identification and treatment.
Administer empiric broad-spectrum antimicrobials within 1 hour for definite/probable sepsis/shock, utilizing prolonged beta-lactam infusions for maintenance.
Initiate at least **30 mL**/kg balanced crystalloids within 3 hours for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion, followed by norepinephrine (target MAP **65 mmHg**, or 60-**65 mmHg** for age ≥ 65).
Start vasopressors peripherally through a high-quality IV if central access is delayed, monitoring closely for extravasation.
For ARDS, use low tidal volume (**6 mL**/kg IBW) and consider HFNC, awake proning, and prone ventilation for moderate-to-severe cases.
Consider IV hydrocortisone (**200 mg**/day) for septic shock requiring vasopressors, but avoid routine IV vitamin C, IVIG, or blood purification.
Strongly recommend pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH), early enteral nutrition, and insulin therapy for glucose levels ≥ **180 mg**/dL.
consensus_method: GRADE methodology, Evidence to Decision (EtD) framework

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
Early identification of sepsis is **critical** to reducing **mortality**. The guidelines recommend health systems implement performance improvement programs that incorporate systematic screening for acutely ill, high-risk patients, standard operating procedures (SOPs) for treatment, and quality improvement (QI) strategies. The use of multidisciplinary "Code Sepsis" or "Sepsis Huddle" protocols is suggested to expedite bedside evaluation and treatment. For prehospital care, the panel suggests using standard sepsis screening tools during ambulance or flight transport, combined with hospital prenotification. In the hospital setting, the guidelines strongly recommend using Early Warning Scores (NEWS, NEWS2, MEWS, or SIRS) over qSOFA as a single screening tool due to qSOFA's poor sensitivity. Sepsis remains a clinical diagnosis; the panel advises against ruling in or ruling out sepsis based on any single biomarker and found insufficient evidence to recommend novel rapid host response diagnostics (e.g., monocyte distribution width, mRNA transcripts, or AI-based immunoscores).

Sepsis QI initiatives are associated with reduced **mortality** (OR 0.66; 95% CI, 0.61-0.72). Prehospital NEWS2 demonstrated the best **absolute** test performance (sensitivity 73.1%; 95% CI, 71.8-74.4%; AUC 0.77; specificity 81.6%). qSOFA had the lowest prehospital sensitivity (23.1%; 95% CI, 21.8-24.3%). Blood culture collection before antimicrobial administration is strongly **recommended**; administering antibiotics prior to culture collection reduces positivity from 31.4% to 19.4% (a 12.0% **absolute** reduction and 38.2% relative reduction in sensitivity).

recommendations.statement: Health systems should implement performance improvement programs incorporating systematic screening, SOPs, and QI strategies for acutely ill, high-risk patients.
Multidisciplinary "Code Sepsis" or "Sepsis Huddle" protocols are suggested to expedite bedside evaluation and treatment.
For prehospital care, use standard sepsis screening tools during transport combined with hospital prenotification.
Strongly recommend using Early Warning Scores (NEWS, NEWS2, MEWS, or SIRS) over qSOFA as a single screening tool in the hospital setting.
Do not rule in or rule out sepsis based on any single biomarker.
There is insufficient evidence to recommend novel rapid host response diagnostics.
Strongly recommend blood culture collection before antimicrobial administration.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
For patients with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock, the guidelines suggest administering at least **30 mL**/kg of IV crystalloid within the first 3 hours. Sepsis-induced hypoperfusion is defined by hypotension (MAP < **65 mmHg**, SBP < **90 mmHg**, or relative SBP drop) or elevated lactate (≥ **4 mmol**/L, though intermediate levels of 2 to **4 mmol**/L also warrant consideration). Weight-based fluid volumes should be calculated using actual body weight, or adjusted/ideal body weight for patients with a BMI > **30 kg**/m2. For patients with persistent hypotension, the panel suggests initial fluid bolus resuscitation followed by vasopressor support, though immediate concurrent vasopressors are warranted in unstable shock. Vasopressors should be started peripherally to restore MAP rather than delaying treatment for central venous access. The **recommended** initial MAP target is **65 mmHg**; however, for patients aged 65 years or older, a lower target of 60-**65 mmHg** is suggested to reduce vasopressor exposure and associated tachyarrhythmias.

Failure to receive **30 mL**/kg of crystalloid within 3 hours is associated with increased in-hospital **mortality**, delayed shock resolution, and prolonged ICU stay. In patients aged 65 years or older, a lower blood pressure target (60-**65 mmHg**) was associated with reduced **mortality** at longest follow-up (RR 0.89; 95% CI, 0.81-0.98). Peripheral vasopressor administration is associated with a pooled complication rate of 5.97% (95% CI, 1.17-14.09%), primarily minor extravasation, when managed under strict safety protocols.

recommendations.statement: For patients with sepsis-induced hypoperfusion or septic shock, suggest administering at least **30 mL**/kg of IV crystalloid within the first 3 hours.
Calculate weight-based fluid volumes using actual body weight, or adjusted/ideal body weight for patients with a BMI > **30 kg**/m2.
For persistent hypotension, suggest initial fluid bolus followed by vasopressor support; immediate concurrent vasopressors are warranted in unstable shock.
Start vasopressors peripherally to restore MAP rather than delaying treatment for central venous access.
The **recommended** initial MAP target is **65 mmHg**.
For patients aged 65 years or older, suggest a lower MAP target of 60-**65 mmHg**.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
Early and appropriate antimicrobial administration is the cornerstone of sepsis **survival**. The guidelines recommend administering empiric antimicrobials immediately, ideally within 1 hour of recognition, for patients with definite or probable septic shock, or definite/probable sepsis without shock. For patients with possible sepsis without shock, a time-limited course of rapid investigation (within 3 hours) is suggested, with antibiotic initiation if concern for infection persists. If the likelihood of infection is low and shock is absent, the panel suggests deferring antimicrobials while closely monitoring the patient. Prehospital antibiotic administration is suggested en route if septic shock is present and transport/medical contact delay exceeds 60 minutes. To optimize pharmacodynamics, the guidelines strongly recommend using prolonged infusions of beta-lactams for maintenance therapy after an initial loading dose. The use of therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) is suggested on a case-by-case basis. Empiric multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogen coverage should be used only in patients at high risk (e.g., prior colonization, prolonged hospitalization in high-prevalence units), and empiric antifungal or anaerobic coverage should be avoided unless specific risk factors are present.

Prolonged infusions of beta-lactams reduce short-term **mortality** compared to bolus administration (RR 0.91; 95% CI, 0.85-0.97, high certainty), translating to 25 fewer deaths per 1,000 patients. Prolonged infusions also increased days alive and out of the ICU (MD 1.5 days; 95% CI, 0.1 fewer to 3.0 more) and out of the hospital (MD 1.8 days; 95% CI, 0.3 to 3.3 more). Empiric MDR coverage in high-risk cohorts suggested a possible reduction in short-term **mortality** (OR 0.71; 95% CI, 0.56-0.92), driven primarily by MRSA data. Empiric antifungal therapy did not show a **significant** reduction in short-term **mortality** (RR 0.93; 95% CI, 0.66-1.32).

recommendations.statement: Administer empiric antimicrobials immediately (ideally within 1 hour) for definite or probable septic shock, or definite/probable sepsis without shock.
For possible sepsis without shock, suggest a time-limited course of rapid investigation (within 3 hours), with antibiotic initiation if concern for infection persists.
If the likelihood of infection is low and shock is absent, suggest deferring antimicrobials while closely monitoring the patient.
Suggest prehospital antibiotic administration en route if septic shock is present and transport/medical contact delay exceeds 60 minutes.
Strongly recommend using prolonged infusions of beta-lactams for maintenance therapy after an initial loading dose.
Suggest therapeutic drug monitoring (TDM) on a case-by-case basis.
Use empiric multidrug-resistant (MDR) pathogen coverage only in patients at high risk.
Avoid empiric antifungal or anaerobic coverage unless specific risk factors are present.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
The guidelines suggest using either invasive or noninvasive blood pressure monitoring, noting that while arterial lines provide continuous data, noninvasive monitoring is acceptable and may avoid complications. Crystalloids are **recommended** as the first-line fluid for resuscitation, with balanced crystalloids (e.g., Lactated Ringer's) suggested over 0.9% normal saline to avoid hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis. Starches are strongly **recommended** against, and gelatins are suggested against. The use of supplemental albumin is suggested only for patients who have received large volumes of crystalloids or have concomitant cirrhosis. To guide ongoing fluid administration beyond the initial resuscitation phase, the panel suggests using dynamic measures of fluid responsiveness (e.g., passive leg raise, stroke volume variation, pulse pressure variation) over physical exam or static measures alone. Norepinephrine is strongly **recommended** as the first-line vasopressor. If escalating doses of norepinephrine are required, vasopressin should be added sequentially, followed by epinephrine. For patients with septic shock and concomitant cardiac dysfunction with persistent hypoperfusion, the addition of dobutamine to norepinephrine or the use of epinephrine alone is suggested. The panel suggests against the routine use of beta-blockers or levosimendan, and found insufficient evidence for oral midodrine or IV methylene blue.

Balanced crystalloids probably reduce **mortality** compared to 0.9% saline (OR 0.94; 95% CI, 0.85-1.04) and reduce the need for new renal replacement therapy (OR 0.86; 95% CI, 0.74-0.99). Albumin showed no effect on 28-day **mortality** (RR 1.01; 95% CI, 0.90-1.14) or 90-day **mortality** compared to crystalloids. Fluid management guided by dynamic measures reduces **mortality** (RR 0.91; 95% CI, 0.79-1.06) and **significantly** reduces the need for RRT (RR 0.75; 95% CI, 0.58-0.98). Adding vasopressin to norepinephrine probably reduces the incidence of atrial fibrillation (RR 0.66; 95% CI, 0.42-1.05) but may increase digital ischemia (RR 2.87; 95% CI, 0.84-9.82).

recommendations.statement: Suggest using either invasive or noninvasive blood pressure monitoring.
Crystalloids are **recommended** as the first-line fluid for resuscitation.
Suggest balanced crystalloids over 0.9% normal saline.
Strongly recommend against starches.
Suggest against gelatins.
Suggest supplemental albumin only for patients who have received large volumes of crystalloids or have concomitant cirrhosis.
Suggest using dynamic measures of fluid responsiveness over physical exam or static measures alone to guide ongoing fluid administration.
Strongly recommend norepinephrine as the first-line vasopressor.
If escalating doses of norepinephrine are required, add vasopressin sequentially, followed by epinephrine.
For septic shock with cardiac dysfunction and persistent hypoperfusion, suggest adding dobutamine to norepinephrine or using epinephrine alone.
Suggest against the routine use of beta-blockers or levosimendan.
There is insufficient evidence for oral midodrine or IV methylene blue.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
For patients with sepsis and acute hypoxemic respiratory failure, the guidelines suggest measuring oxygenation via pulse oximetry (SpO2) or arterial blood gas (SaO2), with a strong caution that pulse oximetry is less accurate in patients in shock, those with darker skin tones (where it overestimates saturation), and at extreme saturation ranges (< 90% or > 97%). Oxygen therapy should be titrated to target either liberal (96-97%) or conservative (90-93%) levels based on patient factors and resources. High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) therapy is suggested over conventional oxygen therapy and over noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) as initial therapy. A trial of awake proning is suggested for non-intubated patients. For patients requiring mechanical ventilation, the guidelines strongly recommend a low tidal volume ventilation strategy (**6 mL**/kg ideal body weight [IBW]) for ARDS, and suggest 6-**8 mL**/kg IBW for patients without ARDS to prevent ventilator-induced lung injury. Plateau pressures should be limited to < 30 cm H2O. Higher PEEP is suggested for moderate-to-severe ARDS, while incremental PEEP titration is strongly **recommended** against. Prone ventilation for > 12 hours daily and intermittent neuromuscular blocking agent (NMBA) boluses are suggested for moderate-to-severe ARDS.

Black patients have nearly three times the frequency of occult hypoxemia compared to White patients due to pulse oximetry overestimation bias. HFNC compared to conventional oxygen therapy showed a potential reduction in **mortality** (RR 0.90; 95% CI, 0.74-1.10) and a possible reduction in the need for intubation (RR 0.88; 95% CI, 0.77-1.00). Awake proning in non-intubated patients reduces the requirement for intubation (RR 0.82; 95% CI, 0.73-0.93). Low tidal volume ventilation (**6 mL**/kg) is a strong recommendation with high certainty of evidence for ARDS, while moderate tidal volumes (6-**8 mL**/kg) are suggested for non-ARDS sepsis patients to mitigate the risk of unrecognized ARDS, which occurs in 48.7% to 52.4% of eligible patients.

recommendations.statement: Suggest measuring oxygenation via pulse oximetry (SpO2) or arterial blood gas (SaO2), with caution regarding pulse oximetry accuracy in certain patients.
Titrate oxygen therapy to target either liberal (96-97%) or conservative (90-93%) levels based on patient factors and resources.
Suggest High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC) therapy over conventional oxygen therapy and over noninvasive positive pressure ventilation (NIPPV) as initial therapy.
Suggest a trial of awake proning for non-intubated patients.
Strongly recommend a low tidal volume ventilation strategy (**6 mL**/kg ideal body weight [IBW]) for ARDS.
Suggest 6-**8 mL**/kg IBW for patients without ARDS to prevent ventilator-induced lung injury.
Limit plateau pressures to < 30 cm H2O.
Suggest higher PEEP for moderate-to-severe ARDS.
Strongly recommend against incremental PEEP titration.
Suggest prone ventilation for > 12 hours daily and intermittent neuromuscular blocking agent (NMBA) boluses for moderate-to-severe ARDS.

recommendation_blocks.narrative:
The guidelines suggest using IV corticosteroids (typically hydrocortisone **200 mg**/day, administered as intermittent doses) for patients with septic shock requiring vasopressor support. The panel suggests against the use of antipyretic therapy (pharmacologic or surface cooling) for the sole purpose of improving clinical outcomes, noting that masking fever may delay diagnosis. The guidelines suggest against the use of IV vitamin C, IV immunoglobulins (IVIG), blood purification techniques (including hemoperfusion, high-dose hemofiltration, plasma exchange, or polymyxin B hemoperfusion), Vitamin D therapy, and IV XueBiJing (outside approved jurisdictions). For supportive care, the panel suggests using proton-pump inhibitors (PPIs) for stress ulcer prophylaxis in patients with risk factors for GI bleeding. Probiotics are suggested against. Active fluid removal (using diuretics or ultrafiltration) is suggested after the acute resuscitation phase once the patient is hemodynamically stable. Restrictive blood transfusion strategies, early enteral nutrition (within 72 hours), and insulin therapy initiated at glucose levels ≥ **180 mg**/dL are **recommended**. Pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH over UFH) is strongly **recommended**.

Corticosteroids in septic shock result in a small reduction in 28-day **mortality** (RR 0.92; 95% CI, 0.83-1.01) and a higher rate of shock reversal at 7 days (RR 1.29; 95% CI, 1.13-1.46), but increase hyperglycemia (RR 1.19; 95% CI, 1.10-1.28) and hypernatremia (RR 1.64; 95% CI, 1.32-2.03). IV vitamin C showed no **mortality** benefit at 90 days in low-risk-of-bias trials (RR 1.06; 95% CI, 0.95-1.18). PPIs for stress ulcer prophylaxis reduce clinically important GI bleeding (RR 0.48; 95% CI, 0.30-0.78) with no **significant** effect on **mortality** (RR 0.99; 95% CI, 0.93-1.05) or pneumonia rates. Probiotics showed no **significant** impact on **mortality** (RR 0.95; 95% CI, 0.87-1.04). Active fluid removal with diuretics only (RR 0.89; 95% CI, 0.78-1.01) showed a more favorable trend than diuretics combined with ultrafiltration (RR 1.13; 95% CI, 0.83-1.53).

recommendations.statement: Suggest using IV corticosteroids (hydrocortisone **200 mg**/day) for patients with septic shock requiring vasopressor support.
Suggest against the use of antipyretic therapy for the sole purpose of improving clinical outcomes.
Suggest against the use of IV vitamin C, IV immunoglobulins (IVIG), blood purification techniques, Vitamin D therapy, and IV XueBiJing (outside approved jurisdictions).
Suggest using proton-pump inhibitors (PPIs) for stress ulcer prophylaxis in patients with risk factors for GI bleeding.
Suggest against probiotics.
Suggest active fluid removal (using diuretics or ultrafiltration) after the acute resuscitation phase once the patient is hemodynamically stable.
Recommend restrictive blood transfusion strategies.
Recommend early enteral nutrition (within 72 hours).
Recommend insulin therapy initiated at glucose levels ≥ **180 mg**/dL.
Strongly recommend pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH over UFH).

bedside_protocol.title: Screening & Triage
bedside_protocol.action: Screen all acutely ill patients using NEWS/NEWS2. If the score is elevated, trigger a "Sepsis Huddle" involving a nurse, physician, and pharmacist. Categorize the patient's sepsis likelihood (Definite, Probable, Possible, Unlikely).

bedside_protocol.title: Diagnostic Workup
bedside_protocol.action: Immediately obtain 2 sets of blood cultures (10 mL per bottle) from separate sites. Measure venous or arterial lactate. Do not delay antimicrobial therapy for more than 45 minutes to obtain cultures if access is difficult.

bedside_protocol.title: Antimicrobial Initiation
bedside_protocol.action: If Definite/Probable Sepsis or Septic Shock is present: Administer broad-spectrum IV empiric antimicrobials within 1 hour of recognition. If Possible Sepsis without Shock is present: Initiate a rapid diagnostic workup (imaging, labs). If infection is confirmed or highly suspected, administer antimicrobials within 3 hours of initial suspicion. Infusion Strategy: Administer a standard loading dose of beta-lactams, followed immediately by a prolonged infusion (extended over at least half the dosing interval, or continuous infusion) for maintenance therapy.

bedside_protocol.title: Fluid Resuscitation
bedside_protocol.action: If the patient exhibits sepsis-induced hypoperfusion (MAP < 65 mmHg, SBP < 90 mmHg, or lactate ≥ 4 mmol/L), initiate a rapid infusion of at least 30 mL/kg of balanced crystalloids (e.g., Lactated Ringer's) within the first 3 hours. For patients with a BMI > 30 kg/m2, calculate the volume using adjusted or ideal body weight.

bedside_protocol.title: Vasoactive Support & Titration
bedside_protocol.action: If hypotension persists after the initial fluid bolus, initiate norepinephrine. Access: Start norepinephrine peripherally through a high-quality, large-bore IV (minimum 20G, preferably in the antecubital fossa) if central access is not immediately available. Monitor the peripheral site hourly for signs of extravasation. Targets: Titrate norepinephrine to maintain a MAP ≥ 65 mmHg. For patients aged ≥ 65 years, target a MAP of 60-65 mmHg. Sequential Escalation: If norepinephrine requirements exceed 0.2-0.3 mcg/kg/min, sequentially add vasopressin (fixed at 0.03 units/min). If MAP remains inadequate, add epinephrine. Inotropic Support: If bedside echocardiography reveals myocardial dysfunction and the patient has persistent hypoperfusion despite adequate fluids and MAP, add dobutamine (titrated from 2.5 to 20 mcg/kg/min) to norepinephrine, or switch to epinephrine monotherapy.

bedside_protocol.title: Adjunctive Therapy
bedside_protocol.action: For patients with septic shock requiring escalating doses of norepinephrine (e.g., > 0.2 mcg/kg/min), initiate IV hydrocortisone 200 mg/day (either 50 mg IV every 6 hours or as a continuous infusion). Monitor blood glucose every 4 hours, and initiate insulin therapy if glucose levels are ≥ 180 mg/dL.

bedside_protocol.title: Resuscitation Monitoring & De-escalation
bedside_protocol.action: Assess fluid responsiveness every 1-2 hours using dynamic measures (e.g., passive leg raise with stroke volume tracking, or pulse pressure variation if mechanically ventilated). Monitor capillary refill time (target ≤ 3 seconds) and serial lactate (target ≥ 10% clearance every 2 hours). Once hemodynamic stability is achieved, transition to active fluid removal using protocolized diuretics to avoid fluid overload and tissue edema. Reevaluate antimicrobial therapy daily; de-escalate or discontinue therapy as soon as microbiological data or clinical improvement permits.

strengths_limitations: The 2026 guidelines are highly robust, utilizing a rigorous GRADE and EtD framework. The panel achieved exceptional geographic and professional diversity, including substantial representation from LMICs (38%) and a dedicated patient and family advisory panel, ensuring recommendations are scientifically sound and pragmatically adaptable. A major limitation is the low or very low certainty of evidence underpinning several core recommendations, including the **30 mL**/kg fluid volume threshold, the timing of antimicrobial administration in possible sepsis, and the safety of peripheral vasopressors. Many recommendations rely heavily on observational data, which are inherently prone to confounding by indication. Furthermore, advanced diagnostic tools and specialized therapies face **significant** implementation barriers in low-resource settings, potentially widening health equity gaps.
